revision.comment: Bot: mark 3 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24644718927|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:祝 (神職)]] '''Hōri''' (祝) refers to individuals who serve deities in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}}. It is a general term for those who offer their services to the gods, Ranking below {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} and {{ill|Negi|ja|禰宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negi|1=Negi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594490}} in the hierarchy. They are also known as '''Hōriko''' (祝子) and '''Hōribe''' (祝部). == Overview == Hōri were among the ancient [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto priests]] who served at {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and were engaged in conducting {{ill|rituals|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rituals|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q132241}}. The earliest recorded Hōri is {{ill|Ōgami no Hiyoshi|ja|大神比義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgami no Hiyoshi|1=Ōga no Higi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119922506}}, who was appointed during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} as the first priest of {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮|1=Usa Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q715632}}, becoming its inaugural {{ill|Ōmiya-no-kami|ja|大宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮司|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453500}}. Various etymologies are suggested for the term: 1. Derived from "Harafu" (to purify). 2. "Ha" meaning "feather," implying the movement of sleeves during sacred dances. 3. Related to "crawling in reverence" before the deities. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, it is recorded that {{ill|Iga-hiko|ja|伊賀彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iga-hiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135579148}} was appointed as a Hōri to enshrine the gods during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|qid=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}}. Additionally, mentions of Hōri serving at shrines in regions such as {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} further confirm the early use of the title. In different provinces: - At {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} ({{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}), positions like ''Ōhōri'' and ''Gonhōri'' existed. - At {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|Aso Shrine|qid=Q715639|lt=Aso Shrine|1=Aso Shrine}} ({{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|qid=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}), titles ranged from ''Ichihōri'' to ''Jūhōri'' (First to Tenth Hōri). - Other notable shrines with historical Hōri roles include {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}, {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|qid=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}}, and {{ill|Kōra taisha|en|Kōra taisha|qid=Q715647|lt=Kōra Taisha|1=Kōra taisha}}. In ancient times, Hōri were also called ''Hōribe''. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|1=Shokuinryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60996852}} mentions them, with descriptions in the {{ill|Ryō no Gige|ja|令義解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no Gige|qq=}} explaining their selection and duties. === In Poetry === The {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} features a {{ill|Sedōka|ja|旋頭歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sedōka|1=sedōka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q678489}} describing a sacred cedar tree worshiped by a Hōri of {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}: {{Quotation|'''御幣取り 三輪の祝が 斎ふ杉原 薪伐り ほとほとしくに 手斧取らえぬ'''
Translation: "Bearing sacred wands, the Hōri of Miwa reveres the sacred cedar grove; as he chops firewood, he nearly loses his axe."|Sedōka (Poem 1403)|''Man'yōshū'', Book VII}} == References == == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} * {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Negi|ja|禰宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negi|1=Negi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594490}} '''Hōri''' (祝, also pronounced as "hōri"The historical kana spelling is "hafuri.") is a general term in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} for individuals who serve deities. It also specifically refers to those Ranked below {{ill|Kannushi|ja|神主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannushi|qq=}} and {{ill|Negi (Shinto)|ja|禰宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negi (Shinto)|1=Negi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594490}} in service to the gods. It is also known as '''Hōriko''' (祝子) or '''Hōribe''' (祝部). == Overview == Hōri has been a position within the {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} priesthood since ancient times, serving at {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and participating in {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q132241}}. The earliest historical mention of Hōri dates to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}, when {{ill|Ōgami Hiye|ja|大神比義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgami Hiye|1=Ōga no Higi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q119922506}} was appointed Japan's first Hōri and became the initial {{ill|Chief Priest (Ōmiya-gū)|ja|大宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest (Ōmiya-gū)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮司|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453500}} of {{ill|Usa Jingū|lt=Usa Jingū|lt=Usa Hachiman-gū|ja|宇佐八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman-gū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇佐神宮}}.Ogawa Shin'ichi, *Usa Jingū to Ōgami-shi*, Bungeisha.Kokugakuin University Japanese Culture Research Institute, *Shinto Jiten*.Nakano Hatanosu, *Hachiman Shinkō*, p. 91.*Kami Iwa Shimizu Monjo*, Hōki 4 (774).*Ōgami-shi Kaden*.*Kōnin Kanpu* (cited in *Kōnin Roku*, 815). The etymology of "Hōri" has been the subject of various theories: 1. Derived from the term "hara(u)" (to cleanse). 2. Related to "ha" (羽, feather), as it is said that performers waved their sleeves in ritual dances. 3. Originating from the verb "haifu" (匍匐在), meaning "to crawl humbly in service to the gods." The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} records that during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|qid=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}}, {{ill|Iga Hiko|ja|伊賀彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iga Hiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135579148}} was appointed Hōri to enshrine the gods. Later references in the same chronicle during the reign of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} mention Hōri serving at shrines in {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, such as the {{ill|Otakesha Shrine|ja|小竹社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otakesha Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579151|1=Otakesha Shrine}} and Amanosha Shrine. Notable examples of Hōri across Japan include: - In {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa-taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} ({{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, present-day Nagano Prefecture), titles such as {{ill|Ōhōri|ja|大祝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhōri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祝 (神職)|1=Hōri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24869239}} and Gonhōri (権祝) existed in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. - In {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|Aso Shrine|qid=Q715639|lt=Aso Shrine|1=Aso Shrine}} ({{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|qid=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}, present-day Kumamoto Prefecture), there were titles such as Ichihōri through Jūhōri (One through Ten Hōri), as well as Kuni no Miyatsuko Hōri and Kanekoboru Hōri. - In {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|qid=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} ({{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|qid=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}}, present-day Ehime Prefecture), an Ōhōri served a central role. - Other examples include the ancient Hōri roles at {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|qid=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}} ({{ill|Hitachi Province|en|Hitachi Province|qid=Q1055353|lt=Hitachi Province|1=Hitachi Province}}) and {{ill|Kōra taisha|en|Kōra taisha|qid=Q715647|lt=Kōra Taisha|1=Kōra taisha}} ({{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|qid=Q864159|lt=Chikugo Province|1=Chikugo Province}}). Historically, Hōri were also referred to as Hōribe (祝部). The {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制|1=Ritsuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q742460}} codified their roles in the {{ill|Shokuinryō|ja|職員令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokuinryō|qq=}}, which stated that: > "The Hōribe (meaning those who act as ritual leaders) shall be selected by provincial governors from among the {{ill|Kambe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kambe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11589706}} (shrines’ hereditary communities). If no suitable candidates exist within the Kambe, individuals may be chosen from the general populace."*Shokuinryō*. Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, Hōribe have continued to serve at the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, where they are tasked with shrine maintenance and safeguarding sacred implements such as the {{ill|御匙|ja|御匙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御匙|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135579154}} and {{ill|御鑰|ja|御鑰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御鑰|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135579157}}. The {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} includes references to Hōri, such as a poem describing the {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}’s Hōri offering {{ill|nusa|ja|幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御幣|1=Gohei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1011372}} at a sacred cedar tree.Example poem from *Man'yōshū* Volume VII. == Notes == == Sources == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Shinto Daijiten Volume 3|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1941|page=168}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Shōkai Kogo Jiten|publisher=Meiji Shoin|year=1988}} {{translated page|ja|祝 (神職)|version=100689899|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:祝_(神職)]] [[ja:祝 (神道)]] [[ja:祝 (神職)]] [[en:Houri]] {{wikidata link|Q24869239}} [[Category:Titles and Positions in Shintō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with deleted QID in ill template]]
